glossary.def: A generative model trained as a two-player minimax game in which a generator turns noise into samples and a discriminator tries to distinguish those samples from real data. The generator is supervised only by gradients flowing back through the discriminator, so the loss function is itself learned and sharpens as the samples improve; its global optimum is reached when the generated and real distributions coincide.

A GAN failure in which the generator concentrates most of its probability mass on a small set of outputs that reliably fool the current discriminator, instead of covering the full diversity of the data. It is a symptom of the unstable, non-stationary optimization that adversarial games are prone to, and a major motivation for alternatives such as Wasserstein GANs.

A training scheme in which an agent generates all of its own training data by playing against copies or past versions of itself, with no external dataset or teacher. Because the opponent improves whenever the agent does, the difficulty rises automatically, producing a self-generating curriculum — the mechanism behind AlphaZero, AlphaStar, and Pluribus.

Estimating the value of a state from the (current, imperfect) value estimates of the states it leads to, so the estimates pull one another toward consistency rather than being supplied as labels. In two-player zero-sum games the backup is a minimax (negamax), and iterating it is a contraction that converges to the game's true values.

A multi-agent RL paradigm in which each agent's critic may observe the full joint state and actions during training — making its learning target stationary — while each agent's actor learns a policy that runs on its own local observation alone at deployment. MADDPG is the canonical instance; it is the standard remedy for the non-stationarity that plagues independent multi-agent learners.

A two-player coordination game with two pure Nash equilibria: hunting a stag together (payoff-dominant) and each hunting hare (risk-dominant, safe). With the payoffs (4,4)/(3,3), cooperation is rational only when you believe your partner cooperates with probability above 0.75 — a threshold, not a payoff comparison, which is why coordination in multi-agent systems is hard.

Two competing criteria for selecting among multiple equilibria in a coordination game. A payoff-dominant equilibrium yields the highest payoff for all players; a risk-dominant one is the safer choice under uncertainty about the partner's action. Learners and populations frequently converge to the risk-dominant (worse) equilibrium when initial beliefs fall below the basin threshold.

A robust-optimization scheme that hardens a model by minimizing its loss on the worst-case perturbation of each input within a small budget — an inner maximization over an epsilon-ball wrapped in an outer minimization over weights. It manufactures a self-generating curriculum of hard examples, but usually trades away clean accuracy and only defends the narrow threat model the perturbation set encodes.

Madry, A. et al. (2018). Towards Deep Learning Models Resistant to Adversarial Attacks. ICLR.
Meta FAIR Diplomacy Team et al. (2022). Human-level play in the game of Diplomacy (CICERO). Science 378.
